questions.question: Prove that √2 is irrational.
questions.answer: Assume √2 = p/q in lowest form. Squaring gives 2q² = p², so p is even. Let p = 2k, then q is also even, contradicting the assumption that p and q are coprime. Hence √2 is irrational.

questions.question: Prove that √3 is irrational.
questions.answer: Assume √3 = p/q. Squaring gives 3q² = p², implying both p and q are divisible by 3, which contradicts the coprime assumption.

questions.question: Using Euclid’s Division Algorithm, find the HCF of 306 and 657.
Explain why the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic is essential in number theory.
questions.answer: It guarantees unique prime factorisation, which is the basis for many results including HCF, LCM and irrationality proofs.

questions.question: Prove that √5 is irrational.
questions.answer: Assume √5 = p/q. Squaring gives 5q² = p², implying both p and q divisible by 5, contradicting coprimality.

questions.question: Explain why Euclid’s Division Algorithm always terminates.
questions.answer: Because the remainders decrease and there are only finitely many positive integers less than a given number.

questions.question: Explain why the HCF × LCM relation does not hold for three numbers.
questions.answer: The relation is derived only for two numbers and does not generalise to three numbers.

questions.question: Prove that √7 is irrational.
questions.answer: Assuming √7 rational leads to a contradiction using prime factorisation, hence √7 is irrational.

questions.question: Explain why irrational numbers cannot be represented as terminating or repeating decimals.
questions.answer: Terminating or repeating decimals can be expressed as fractions, which contradicts irrationality.

questions.question: Using prime factorisation, find the LCM of 72 and 120.
Explain why p | a² implies p | a.
questions.answer: Because the prime factorisation of a² contains each prime factor of a twice.

questions.question: Explain why √12 is irrational.
questions.answer: √12 = 2√3 and since √3 is irrational, √12 is irrational.

questions.question: Explain why 2 is the only even prime number.
questions.answer: All other even numbers are divisible by 2 and hence composite.

questions.question: Explain the necessity of real numbers.
questions.answer: Real numbers represent all points on the number line, including irrational quantities.

questions.question: Explain why √49 is rational.
questions.answer: √49 = 7, which is an integer.

questions.question: Explain why irrational × rational is irrational.
questions.answer: Multiplying by a rational number does not eliminate the irrational part.

questions.question: Explain the role of contradiction in proving irrationality.
questions.answer: Contradiction shows that the assumption of rationality leads to an impossible situation.

questions.question: Explain why prime factorisation is unique.
questions.answer: Because primes are indivisible and form the basic building blocks of numbers.

questions.question: Using Euclid’s algorithm, find the HCF of 867 and 255.
Explain why √8 is irrational.
questions.answer: √8 = 2√2 and √2 is irrational, so √8 is irrational.

questions.question: Explain why terminating decimals correspond to factors 2 and 5 only.
questions.answer: Because only powers of 10 (2 × 5) produce terminating decimals.

questions.question: Explain why HCF of two coprime numbers is 1.
questions.answer: Coprime numbers have no common factors other than 1.

questions.question: Explain why irrational numbers are needed in mathematics.
questions.answer: They represent quantities that cannot be expressed as fractions, such as roots and π.

questions.question: Explain why √25 is rational.
questions.answer: √25 = 5, which is an integer.

questions.question: Explain the importance of Euclid’s Division Algorithm in mathematics.
questions.answer: It provides a systematic method to find the HCF of numbers and supports number theory proofs.
